note: Common reef-safe community fish. adultSizeIn = typical adult length; minTankGal = widely-cited minimum for long-term health (nominal tank size, not water volume); bioloadFactor = waste output per inch relative to a 1.0 baseline (active/messy eaters run higher). Sizes and minimums are hobby consensus (LiveAquaria / Reef2Reef), not lab figures.

fish.name: Ocellaris Clownfish
fish.careNote: Keep as a bonded pair or a single — two unbonded adults will fight.

fish.name: Diamond Watchman Goby
fish.careNote: Sand sifter — will constantly spit sand onto low corals.

fish.name: Firefish
fish.careNote: Shy jumper — needs a lid and calm tankmates.

fish.name: Lawnmower Blenny
fish.careNote: Eats film algae — needs an established tank to graze.

fish.name: Banggai Cardinalfish
fish.careNote: Best as a single or a bonded pair; a loose group needs space.

fish.name: Green Chromis
fish.careNote: Shoal in odd groups of 5+ — small groups pick each other off one by one.

fish.name: Azure Damsel
fish.careNote: Gets territorial with age — add last.

fish.name: Six Line Wrasse
fish.careNote: Becomes a bully in small tanks — add last, watch peaceful tankmates.

fish.name: Melanurus Wrasse
fish.careNote: Sand sleeper — needs a sand bed and a tight lid.

fish.name: Coral Beauty Angelfish
fish.careNote: Dwarf angel — may nip LPS/clam mantles (reef-safe with caution).

fish.name: Flame Angelfish
fish.careNote: Dwarf angel — reef-safe with caution; one per tank.

fish.name: Yellow Tang
fish.careNote: Needs horizontal swim room, not just volume — 4ft+ tank.

fish.name: Kole Tang
fish.careNote: Best algae-grazing tang for a mid-size reef; still wants a 4ft tank.

fish.name: Blue Hippo Tang
fish.careNote: Gets large and ich-prone — a serious long-term commitment.

fish.name: Lyretail Anthias
fish.careNote: Needs multiple daily feedings and a harem (1 male : several females).

fish.name: Longnose Hawkfish
fish.careNote: Will eat ornamental shrimp and small gobies — not shrimp-safe.

fish.name: Mandarin Dragonet
fish.careNote: Eats live copepods — only for a mature tank with a healthy pod population.
